title: Options as a Strategic Investment
subtitle: Fifth Edition
author: Lawrence G. McMillan
excerpt: The definitive reference for options trading strategies, covering everything from basic calls and puts to advanced volatility-based techniques. Lawrence McMillan provides a comprehensive framework for understanding options pricing, the Greeks, volatility analysis, and position management based on four decades of professional trading experience.
keyIdeas: Options are flexible instruments that can be tailored to any market outlook — bullish, bearish, or neutral — through the selection of strike prices, expiration dates, and strategy combinations
Implied volatility is the single most important factor in options pricing; understanding when it is high or low relative to historical volatility determines whether a strategy will succeed
The Greeks — delta, gamma, theta, vega — are not academic abstractions but essential tools for managing position risk and adjusting strategies as market conditions change
Position sizing and risk management matter more than strategy selection; even the best trade setup fails without proper capital allocation and defined exit rules
Seasoned traders use technical analysis of the underlying stock or index to time entries and exits, combining chart patterns with options-specific metrics
Tax implications of options trading are significant and often overlooked — holding periods, wash sales, and the 60/40 rule for Section 1256 contracts directly affect net returns
keyTakeaways: Calls and puts are the building blocks; all complex strategies are combinations of these basic positions
Implied volatility mean-reverts — buy options when IV is low, sell when IV is high
Time decay accelerates as expiration approaches; theta is the option seller's best friend and the buyer's worst enemy
Vertical spreads limit risk and reduce capital requirements compared to naked positions
The risk of early assignment on American-style options is real, especially on dividend-paying stocks and deep in-the-money positions
Covered calls and cash-secured puts are the safest income-generating strategies for beginners
whoShouldRead: Individual investors who want to move beyond basic stock trading to incorporate options as a strategic tool
Traders preparing for the Series 7 or other professional examinations who need comprehensive options coverage
Financial advisors and portfolio managers who recommend or implement options-based strategies for clients
Experienced stock traders who have lost money on options and want to understand what went wrong
whoShouldSkip: Complete beginners with no understanding of stocks, markets, or basic finance — this book assumes working knowledge of markets
Quantitative professionals seeking complex mathematical models — McMillan focuses on practical application, not theory
Traders looking for a 'system' or guaranteed returns — this book emphasises risk management over promises of quick profits
